questions.question: Explain how blockchain technology can be applied in supply chain management. List at least three specific benefits.
keyPoints: Immutable record of product journey from origin to consumer.
Transparency allowing all stakeholders to verify provenance.
Rapid traceability for contamination or recall events.
Counterfeit detection through verified product history.
Example: Walmart uses blockchain to trace food origin in seconds instead of days.

questions.question: Discuss any four real-world applications of blockchain technology across different industries. For each, explain the specific problem blockchain solves and which blockchain properties (immutability, decentralization, transparency, smart contracts) are leveraged.
keyPoints: Finance/DeFi: Decentralized lending, borrowing, and trading without banks. Leverages smart contracts and decentralization.
Healthcare: Secure, interoperable medical records sharing. Leverages immutability and cryptographic access control.
Supply Chain: Product traceability from origin to consumer. Leverages transparency and immutability.
Voting: Tamper-proof, auditable election systems. Leverages immutability and transparency.
For each: identify the specific problem solved and the blockchain property used.

questions.question: What is Decentralized Finance (DeFi)? Explain how it differs from traditional finance and list three types of DeFi applications.
keyPoints: DeFi provides financial services on blockchain using smart contracts without intermediaries.
Differs from traditional finance by removing banks, brokers, and centralized control.
Types: Decentralized exchanges (DEX), lending/borrowing protocols, yield farming/staking.
Benefits include permissionless access, transparency, and composability.

questions.question: Explain the concept of Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) using blockchain and how it differs from centralized identity management systems.
keyPoints: SSI allows individuals to own and control their identity data on blockchain.
Uses verifiable credentials that can be selectively shared.
Differs from centralized systems where a government or corporation controls identity data.
Eliminates single points of failure and reduces identity theft risk.
